items.q: What is the relationship between the gradients of two perpendicular lines?
items.correct: Their product is -1 — each is the negative reciprocal of the other.
items.misconception: They are equal because perpendicular lines are 'similar' in direction.
items.random_wrong: One is the negative of the other (e.g. 3 and -3).
items.why: Students remember 'negative' but drop the 'reciprocal' part, leading them to negate without flipping.

items.q: On a distance-time graph, what does a horizontal line mean?
items.correct: The object is stationary — distance is not changing, so speed is zero.
items.misconception: The object is moving at a constant speed.
items.why: Students confuse distance-time graphs with speed-time graphs; a horizontal line on the latter would mean constant speed.

items.q: If two lines have the same gradient, what is true about them?
items.correct: They are parallel (or the same line if their intercepts are also equal).
items.misconception: They cross each other at the y-intercept.
items.why: Students don't internalise that gradient determines direction, so lines with equal gradients can never meet (unless identical).

items.q: A line has gradient 3. What is the gradient of a line perpendicular to it?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: −1/3 (about −0.33). “−3” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which line is parallel to y = 2x + 5?
items.why: Correct answer: y = 2x − 1. “y = −2x + 5” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is the x-coordinate of the midpoint of (3, 8) and (11, 2)?
items.why: Exact answer: 7. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
